coredata.dc:description:
Nanofluids are known to have immense potential for heat transfer applications because of their unique thermophysical properties when compared to the conventional heat transfer fluid. Predicting the thermophysical features like thermal conductivity has posed a challenge to their application. This article addresses some of the challenges posed in their prediction by using data sets from several experimental research on various hybrid nanofluids to train an intelligent neural network. The thermal conductivity of hybrid nanofluids is predicted using seven different input variables namely, volume concentration, temperature, the acentric factor of the base fluid, nanoparticle bulk density, mixture ratio of particles, the thermal conductivity, and size of nanoparticles. 715 experimental data points from studies using different hybrid nanoparticles are used in developing a multi-layer perceptron artificial neural network (ANN) and support vector regression (SVR) models. The performance validation of the models is computed using the mean square error (MSE) and the coefficient of determination (R
2 ). The performance result showed an R
2 value of 0.99997 and 0.99788 in the validation phase of the ANN and SVR model, respectively. This indicates that the models are capable of accurately predicting the thermal conductivity of hybrid nanofluids over a wide range of hybrid nanoparticle combinations. Finally, a universal formula using MLP-ANN for predicting the thermal conductivity of hybrid nanofluids is presented.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Nanofluids are known to have immense potential for heat transfer applications because of their unique thermophysical properties when compared to the conventional heat transfer fluid. Predicting the thermophysical features like thermal conductivity has posed a challenge to their application. This article addresses some of the challenges posed in their prediction by using data sets from several experimental research on various hybrid nanofluids to train an intelligent neural network. The thermal conductivity of hybrid nanofluids is predicted using seven different input variables namely, volume concentration, temperature, the acentric factor of the base fluid, nanoparticle bulk density, mixture ratio of particles, the thermal conductivity, and size of nanoparticles. 715 experimental data points from studies using different hybrid nanoparticles are used in developing a multi-layer perceptron artificial neural network (ANN) and support vector regression (SVR) models. The performance validation of the models is computed using the mean square error (MSE) and the coefficient of determination (R
2 ). The performance result showed an R
2 value of 0.99997 and 0.99788 in the validation phase of the ANN and SVR model, respectively. This indicates that the models are capable of accurately predicting the thermal conductivity of hybrid nanofluids over a wide range of hybrid nanoparticle combinations. Finally, a universal formula using MLP-ANN for predicting the thermal conductivity of hybrid nanofluids is presented.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanofluids have the potential to revolutionize the fields of engineering and material science. The dispersions of nanoparticles into fluids have shown to possess distinctive properties which can improve the efficiencies of current engineering systems. However, predicting the thermodynamic properties of nanofluids has been a bit of a quagmire and has limited their applications.

Since the very end of the 19th century, there have been formulas to predict the thermal conductivity of spherical dispersions in a fluid. Maxwell predicted that the thermal conductivity of a fluid that contains solid conducting particles will depend on the thermal conductivity of the base fluid, the thermal conductivity of the solid conducting dispersion, and the volume concentration of the dispersion in the fluid [1]. While the values predicted by Maxwell are relatively accurate for conventional nanofluids like Al2O3 water-based nanofluid and show a similar trend line when compared to experimental results; significant deviations occur when Maxwell model is compared to experimental thermal conductivity values for hybrid nanofluids. There have been other models to predict this thermophysical property. Some models like Hamilton-Crosse Model [2], Wasp Model [3], and Yu and Choi [4] models modified the Maxwell model to improve its predictive accuracy. However, these models still deviate significantly when predicting the thermal conductivity of conventional and hybrid nanofluid.

These deviations are likely because most of the equations proposed do not account for all the variables that affect the thermal conductivity of nanofluids. These variables include base fluid thermal conductivity, nanoparticle thermal conductivity, the shape of the constituent nanoparticle, particle aggregation over time, volume concentration of the nanoparticle, interfacial layer within the nanofluid, particle mixture ratio for hybrid nanofluids, the temperature of the nanofluids, etc. Each of these variables has a unique relationship with the thermal conductivity of the nanofluid and with each other. For instance, the higher the thermal conductivity of the base fluid and constituent nanoparticle the higher the overall thermal conductivity of the fluid. Also, the smaller the particle sizes of nanoparticles in the fluid the higher the thermal conductivity of the fluid, and the higher the rate of particle aggregation over time. It can also be observed that an increase in volume concentration and temperature of nanofluids tends to improve the overall thermal conductivity of the fluid. While many of these relationships can be obtained from experiments on different nanofluids the exact extents to these relationships have not been fully explored.

In recent years, many researchers have used regression correlation equations to reflect the relationship between the thermal conductivity of nanofluids and these variables. Regression correlation equations have been proposed for several nanofluids; these nanofluids include Al2O3 water nanofluid, TiO2 water nanofluid, Al2O3 ethylene glycol (EG) nanofluid, Al2O3 water(40%)–EG(60%) nanofluid [5], Multiwalled carbon nanotube (MWCNT) water nanofluid, graphene oxide-Al2O3 water nanofluid [6], SiC-CuO/C EG nanofluid [7], CNT-Fe3O4 water nanofluid [8], Al2O3- MWCNTs EG nanofluid [9], Al2O3-Fe water nanofluid [10], Al2O3 10w40-engine oil nanofluid [11], Al2O3-TiO2 water nanofluid [12], TiO2-ZnO EG nanofluid [13], MgO-TiO2 water nanofluid [14] and many others [15,16]. These regression correlation equations mainly reflect the relationship between the thermal conductivity of nanofluid, volume concentration of nanoparticles, and temperature. While these regression equations show significantly less deviation compared to classical models they are significantly limited in range. The regression equations are accurate within the range in which the thermal conductivity measurements were taken.

More recently many researchers have turned to artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning models to provide a more accurate predictive model for the thermal conductivity of nanofluids; these models include Multilayer perceptron neural network (MLP), Radial basis function neural networks (RBF) [17], Least square support vector machine (LSSVM), Adaptive-Network Based Fuzzy Inference Systems (ANFIS) [17] genetic algorithm-polynomial neural network (GA-ANN) [18], FCM-based neuro-fuzzy inference system (FCM-ANFIS), Conjugate hybrid-PSO ANFIS (CHPSO ANFIS), ANFIS-PSO [19] and ANFIS-GA [19].

ANFIS, MLP-ANN, RBF-ANN, and LSSVM techniques were used to predict the thermal conductivity of TiO2 nanofluid. The input parameters were taken from several experimental measurements obtained from different published articles [20]. These input parameters include temperature, particle volume concentration, and average particle size. The temperature range considered was between 5 °C to 90 °C, particle volume concentration range was between 0 and 4% and the range for TiO2 particle size was between 0 and 220 nm. Of all the considered techniques the LSSVM showed the highest ability to predict the thermal conductivity of nanofluids containing TiO2 nanoparticles. Adaptive neuro-fuzzy inference system (ANFIS) and least square support vector machine (LSSVM) were applied in predicting the thermal conductivity of conventional nanofluids using temperature, the volume fraction of the particle, the thermal conductivity of the base fluid, the thermal conductivity of the nanoparticles and nanoparticle size as the input variables. In the study 1109 data points were collected from 29 conventional nanofluids [21].

An MLP-ANN was applied in predicting the thermal conductivity of Cu-TiO2 water/EG hybrid nanofluid. The input parameters considered were particle volume concentration and temperature. Results show that the best performing MLP-ANN configuration had an R
2 value of 0.999 and a mean square error (MSE) of 0.00002 [22]. An MLP-ANN was also used to predict the thermal conductivity of SWCNT–Al2O3 EG nanofluid, 40 data points were applied to the ANN model and the results observed that when predicted values are compared to experimentally obtained values a maximum deviation of 1.94% was observed [23].

Support vector regression (SVR) and support vector classification (SVC) are both subsets of support vector machines [27]. SVR was first proposed by Vapnik, and as implied in the name, SVR is used for regression problems and has been applied to many engineering tasks. The concept of the SVR model is to map the non-linear relationship between input and output variables. Hence, the goal of the SVR model is to predict the function from an observed function with minimal error [27].

In theory, the SVR model fits a regression model y = f(x) to the ε − SVR model, to make an accurate prediction of target {y

In overcoming the complexity in computation and high dimensional featured space, kernel functions are defined in SVR models. Kernel functions are either symmetric, positive, or semi-definite [30]. Different kernel functions models have been suggested in literature, although the most utilized are the linear, polynomial, sigmoid, and radial basis functions [31]. The functions of the linear kernel, polynomial kernel, and radial basis function kernel are(x × y),(⌈(x × x
i ) + 1⌉ d ), exp{−y|x − x
i |2} respectively [31].
In this study, the cross-validation method is used, and a polynomial kernel function is adopted to define the non-linear mapping from the input space to some high dimensional feature space.

Traditional modeling is limited in finding relationships between input and output data, as there are scenarios where the relationships are either complex or non-linear. Artificial neural networks are known to be an efficient algorithm for mapping input-output data. The neural network reproduces the functionality of the human brain, in other to learn the relationship that exists among the corresponding variables [32,33]. The application of artificial neural networks (ANN) spans diverse fields [34–36]. Some of the applications of ANN include pattern recognition, classification, estimation, and so on [33]. The original idea of the ANN is to compute and analyze problems in the same pattern as a working human brain. The architecture of ANN is based on the interconnection of units termed “artificial neurons” (similar in pattern to the axons and neurons in the brain). The perceptrons, which consist of the inputs, outputs, and hidden layers are the interconnected structures that provide the basis for the algorithm model [37]. The multilayer-layer perceptron (MLP) network is one of the most used ANN architecture [38]. It is designed to function similarly to the learning process of the human brain. The different learning algorithms of ANN models include Levenberg-Marquard backpropagation (LM), scaled conjugate gradient (SCG), Bayesian regularization (BR), and resilient propagation (RP). Primarily, the ANN architecture consists of input layers, hidden layers, and output layers. The neurons in one layer do not have a relationship with each other [39]. Signals from one layer are transferred to the next layer through a non-linear transfer function. A challenge in utilizing the MLP algorithm is getting an optimal model. This can however be resolved by using different choices of the MLP algorithm to arrive at an efficient model [33]. In the MLP algorithm, the weighted sum of the input is linked to the hidden neurons which is then transformed by the activation function. The input to the output neuron is the output of the hidden neurons, where further transformation takes place. Eq. (4) gives the output layer of the MLP neural network [32];
(4)

ij is the bias of the i neuron in the j layer. The w

i is the input vector and the f
j is the non-linear activation function which may be binary sigmoid, bipolar sigmoid, Gaussian, identity function, and linear function [40].

The schematic representation of the MLP neural network is shown in Fig. 1 . The most utilized transfer function for the output layer is the purelin, while the tangent sigmoid (tansig) and logarithmic sigmoid (logsig) hyperbolic functions are the most used for the hidden layers [41]. In the training process, to minimize errors, there is iterative random initialization of the weight values and bias values. The backpropagation method is usually employed to minimize the errors at every iteration by back-propagating the errors to reduce the values of the weight and bias errors [41].

One of the most important considerations in machine learning regression modeling is how a model generalizes to unseen data, hence a major issue in ANN modeling is over-fitting [42]. This is the inability of a model to predict new datasets, though it fits known datasets in the training process. Several methods have been suggested to avoid this problem, such as model selection, data augmentation, use of regularization techniques, and early stopping method [43].

In this study, the early stopping technique is used to improve the generalization of the model and to eliminate the problem of overfitting. This technique involves splitting the dataset into subsets of training, testing, and validation. The test set is used in the verification of the generalization and accuracy of the trained model [44]. This is vital because at the later stage of the prediction, accuracy begins to worsen and at this point, the model is stopped and then retrained, as this is likely to cause overfitting.

The training algorithms used in this study are the scaled conjugate gradient (SCG), Levenberg-Marquardt (LM), Bayesian regularization (BR), and Resilient backpropagation (RP). The retrieved dataset is split into training and validation dataset. The validation dataset was not used in the training and testing process.

The performance of the model is tested with two different ANN architecture, these are the ANN models with one layer and with two hidden layers. In both structures, the number of neurons is gradually increased to find the optimum.

Table 1 shows the performance parameters used for learning in the ANN model. In this study, the logistic sigmoid is used as the neuron activation function as shown in Eq. (5) and the hidden and output layer is activated by the Purelin function as shown in Eq. (6). The Purelin function is chosen as the activation function for the output neuron because it is ideal for continuous-valued targets [45]. The testing dataset is used in examining the generalization of the model. Fig. 2 outlines the steps taken to obtain the optimum architecture.

The MATLAB 2018a environment is used in carrying out the computation in this study. To model the relative thermal conductivity of hybrid nanofluids the following variables were considered, temperature, volume fraction, mixture ratio, the acentric factor of the base fluid, nanoparticle's thermal conductivity, nanoparticle's density, and nanoparticle's size.

For faster training and to avoid getting stuck at a local optimum, preprocessing of the dataset is important. Before training and testing, all data are normalized between [0–1] and are de-normalized afterward. Eqs. (7) and (8) determine the normalization and denormalization functions, respectively.

The studies that have investigated the relative thermal conductivity of hybrid nanofluids were reviewed and 715 experimental data were obtained from 20 different literatures [6,23,47–64] and used for designing and validating the performance of the predictive model. The results of these studies indicate the factors that primarily affect the thermal conductivity of the nanofluids. These factors are temperature, particle size, surfactants, volume concentration, nanoparticle's shape, size, interfacial layers, mixture ratio, inter-particle spacing, and nanoparticle's thermal conductivity. Also, the thermal conductivity of hybrid nanofluid is influenced by the nanoparticle mixture ratio and the type of base fluid. To make a comprehensive model covering different hybrid nanofluids, the most significant factors that influence thermal conductivity must be recognized. In the present study, the temperature, the volume concentration, the particle size, the mixture ratio, the thermal conductivity of nanoparticle, the nanoparticle density and the acentric factor of base fluid were selected as input parameters. To improve the accuracy of the computing model the acentric factor of the base fluid was used to identify the different base fluids within the study. For the critical properties of mixtures, the acentric factor is determined using Kay's equation as used in the study conducted by Khalif and Vaferi [24]. Table 2 shows the general specification of the data set used in the ANN and support vector model development.

Fig. 3 shows the retrieved hybrid nanofluid from reviewed studies used to predict the thermal conductivity of the different nanofluids individually. Also, Table 3 shows the number of data points selected from each study. The selected data points are within the temperature and volume concentration ranges considered in this study. The retrieved dataset is split into two, 80% for training, and 20% for validation. The 20% validation dataset, representing 143 data points randomly selected from the literature [59,61,63,64] [55], was not used in the training process but was used as an external validator.

In this section, the results from SVR, ANN-BR, ANN-LM, ANN-SCG, and ANN-RP were discussed and analyzed. Also, this section compares the results from all the applied prediction techniques and compares them with each other to determine the techniques with the best performance. Ultimately the relative error in prediction is determined for each technique.

As earlier stated, the SVR model is constructed using the input combinations of temperature, volume concentration, nanoparticle size, mixture ratio, the acentric factor of the base fluid, nanoparticle density, and nanoparticle thermal conductivity. High prediction and generalizability are only ensured by searching for the optimal parameters of the SVR model [25]. The kernel function used in this study is the polynomial function as it gave the best performance. The type of polynomial function used in this study is the cubic polynomial. The kernel contains a bias term b, accommodated within the kernel function. The accuracy of the SVR model is defined here in terms of the generalization ability. In practice, this is inferred as cross-validation (CV) over the training set of known data. The most common CV is the k-fold CV which is usually set between 5 and 10 [65]. The k-fold cross validation used in this study is set to 5. The error between the predicted and real values in a high-dimensional space is the epsilon (ε). The box constraint is described as a parameter penalizing vectors laying outside the epsilon-margin. Varying the box constraint values, increases, or decreases the support vectors. An optimum box constraint value allows for more regularization, as more points lie within the margin and this reduces the risk of overfitting. The points within the margin are support vectors. The optimum parameters for the SVR models are shown in Table 4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the proposed SVR model
ce:section.ce:para:
The accuracy of the SVR model was assessed using the MSE and the coefficient of determination (R
2 ) values between the predicted and the experimental relative thermal conductivity results. From Eqs. (9) and (10), the values for both the MSE and R 2 are obtained for assessing the accuracy of the model in predicting the relative thermal conductivity of the hybrid nanofluid, the statistical and graphical result is shown in Table 5 and Figs. 4 and 5
. Table 5 shows the statistical results retrieved for the training and validation phase of the model. The result shows a high correlation coefficient and low MSE values. This is an indication of the high computational performance of the developed model for relative thermal conductivity prediction.

From Table 5, the R 2 value of 0.99883 in the training dataset shows a high correlation coefficient between the experimental and predicted values during the training process. However, the R 2 value of the validation data set was calculated to be 0.99783. While this is 0.001 lower than the value obtained from the training set, it still reflects an acceptable performance of the prediction model. The validation process confirms the accuracy of the prediction model. Fig. 4b gives a graphical illustration of the validation model.

Fig. 5a and Fig. 5b show the relative error of the training and validation data, respectively. The result shows that the error was neatly spread around the zero line, and this points to the excellence of SVR as a predictive model for the thermal conductivity of nanofluids.

Studies have shown that a small number of neurons in the hidden layers can make the performance of the ANN model unsatisfactory, likewise too large number of neurons can also cause overfitting in the model [32]. In finding an optimum configuration, there is no general rule, as the model configuration would depend on the complexity of the system. The different MLP architecture utilized in this study was examined using trials and errors to obtain the best configuration [66].

Table 6 shows the statistical results retrieved for the training and validation phase of the model. An ANN with one (1) hidden layer and an ANN with two (2) hidden layers were selected. A range of neurons was modeled to determine the optimum performing configuration.

The optimization of the training algorithm is first determined. For optimal training process results, the training process for each architecture is carried out three times, and the average value is computed. Also, based on literature [32], the trial and error method is used in the training algorithm, as regards the number of neurons in the hidden layers, in determining the best network architecture. In this study, the number of hidden layers and the number of neurons in the hidden layers are varied from one to two hidden layers. As observed from Table 6 the optimum MLP configuration was achieved with two hidden layers having 10 neurons in the first hidden layer and 15 neurons in the second hidden layer. The Bayesian regularization (BR) method was used in the training process of the relative thermal conductivity prediction model. The choice of BR was made by investigating previous literature [67–71] which shows that the Bayesian method can solve the challenge posed by overfitting effectively.

While BR has some advantages, according to Okut et al. [67], the performance of BR algorithm may not be significantly better than the other training algorithms, hence this study also utilized the RP, LM, and SCG training algorithms on the determined optimum MLP configuration to validate the best prediction accuracy of the model. The result in Table 7 shows the statistical performance of different training techniques in predicting the relative thermal conductivity of hybrid nanofluids. Based on the recorded results, it is obtained that the BR training algorithm is the best predictive training model; having both the highest R 2 value and least MSE values compared to RP, SCG, and LM. Of the considered training algorithms, the SCG performed the worst. This can be attributed to the model challenges with initial minimization.

Fig. 6a shows the agreement between the predicted and experimental data used in the training process. The R 2 value of 0.99989 in the training process shows a high correlation coefficient. Also, as can be observed from Fig. 6a, all the data points are aligned along the diagonal line of the graph, which shows a high-performance prediction model. Fig. 6b shows the R 2 value of the validation dataset of 0.99993 also confirming a high prediction accuracy. Furthermore, Fig. 7 shows the residual analysis of the training and validation model. The concentration of the errors around the zero line also proves that the high performance of the models.

The universal formula for the relative thermal conductivity of hybrid nanofluid using the optimum MLP model is derived in this study. Eq. (11) presents this universal ANN formula.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparative evaluation of the intelligent models to classical predictive models
ce:section.ce:para:
From the previous sections, MLP and SVR models were developed for the prediction of the relative thermal conductivity of hybrid nanofluids. Table 8 shows that the MLP has a higher prediction accuracy as compared to the SVR model in this study. The Maxwell model for hybrid nanocomposite [72] given in Eqs. (12) and (13) is used in computing the relative thermal conductivity of the validation dataset of the hybrid nanofluid and compared with the machine learning models.

Table 9 shows model accuracy using the experimental results obtained from Al2O3/Cu water hybrid nanofluid at a 50%–50% mixture ratio. From Table 9, it can be observed that the Maxwell model has a higher error and deviation from the actual values, as volume fraction increases. This is in agreement with comparative studies done between experimental values of hybrid nanofluid relative thermal conductivity compared with results obtained by Maxwell's equation [10,73]. Also, the results presented validate the ANN–BR as the most accurate thermal conductivity model presented.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Relationship between different parameters with the relative thermal conductivity of hybrid nanofluids
ce:section.ce:para: In this section, the effect of the different parameters on the thermal conductivity of nanofluids is analyzed. These parameters include volume fraction, mixture ratio, and size of the nanoparticles.

The dataset separated for validation was used in analyzing the effect of some of the considered parameters on the relative thermal conductivity of the fluids. TiO2-SiO2 EG/water (60:40) nanofluid as used in Hamid et al. [53] experiment was mainly used to validate and compare with the proposed intelligent models.

Fig. 8 shows the effect of volume fraction on the effective thermal conductivity of the hybrid nanofluid. The result shows that by only considering the effect of volume concentration the intelligent predictive models to a degree of accuracy match the experimental data. The MLP with BR learning had the best agreement with the experimental data. However, SVR also has a near-perfect prediction as well, and only slightly overestimates thermal conductivity values as the volume concentration of the nanofluid increases. The Maxwell model, on the other hand, shows a significant deviation from the experimental data. This proves the superiority of machine learning algorithms over Maxwell's model.

Table 10 shows how the mixture ratio of the nanoparticles within the hybrid nanofluid influences the effective thermal conductivity of the nanofluid. The measured values of the thermal conductivity of hybrid nanofluids are compared to the forecasted values for thermal conductivity as the mixture ratio of Al2O3 in the Al2O3-ZnO hybrid nanofluid is varied. Both the MLP model and the SVR model predict the curve-like relationship between the mixture ratio and thermal conductivity. However, the Maxwell model inaccurately shows a linear relationship between the mixture ratio and thermal conductivity. The MLP is again more accurate than the SVR models in predicting this relationship.

The result obtained in this study corroborates with those in literature. The study by Kavitha and Kumar [74] compares the performance of the SVR and MLP models in the prediction of the thermal properties of nanofluids. Their study showed that the MLP model performed better than the SVR model, which had a cubic polynomial kernel as the best kernel function. However, the R 2 value obtained in this study showed a better prediction performance when compared with that obtained in their study. A similar study by Jamei et al. [26] used three models; genetic algorithm, model tree, and multiple linear regression for prediction of thermal conductivity of hybrid nanofluid. Their best performing model was the genetic algorithm with an R 2 value of 0.9028 which is less accurate when compared to the results obtained from this study. A study by Wang et al. [66] also compared the prediction performance of hybrid nanofluids using different machine learning algorithms. Their result showed that the best performance was obtained with the mind evolutionary algorithm back propagation neural network (MEA-BPNN), which gave an R2 value of 0.9997. This also shows that the prediction in this study has better accuracy.

To aid in enhancing the thermal application of nanofluids in engineering systems, this study seeks to address the challenges faced with predicting the thermal conductivity of hybrid nanofluids. The study presents an intelligent based model for predicting the thermal conductivity of various hybrid nanofluids. The network uses seven different input variables namely, volume concentration, temperature, the acentric factor of the base fluid, nanoparticle density, mixture ratio of particles, the thermal conductivity, and size of nanoparticles. 715 data points from several published articles are used to validate and train the neural network model. Upon the statistical validation of the models using their R 2 and MSE values, the following conclusions can be reached:
•
When BR, RP, LM, and SCG training algorithms are used to train the MLP, the BR training produced the most accurate hybrid thermal conductivity prediction results when validated.
•
Using the SVR model to predict the thermal conductivity of nanofluids shows fairly accurate results. However, the SVR model does not perform as well as the MLP.
•
The Maxwell model is not an accurate predictor of the thermal conductivity for hybrid nanofluid. It fails to predict the trend lines especially when the mixture ratio of the particles is considered.
•
Both MLP and SVR can accurately predict the effects of volume concentration, mixture ratio, and temperature on the thermal conductivity of hybrid nanofluids.

This research ultimately shows that intelligent systems can help scientists and engineers predictive the thermal conductivity of hybrid nanofluids to a remarkably high degree of accuracy across different base fluid types, nanoparticle types, temperatures, volume concentrations, and mixture ratios within hybrid.
